action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: boat, hunter, hill, king, ship, road, farmer, stone
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): find, hold, watch, see, carry
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a boat find a hunter
    three boats find a hunter

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    two farmers find two ships  =>  hu gisepu hu ragopu gonaro
    three boats carry two hills  =>  musu pumepu hu mipepu rorumo
    three boats watch three hunters  =>  musu pumepu musu gepapu pera
    two hills find two roads  =>  hu mipepu hu nahanapu gonaro
    a road carry two hills  =>  nahana hu mipepu rorumo
    a king carry three roads  =>  mamome musu nahanapu rorumo
    two hills carry a stone  =>  hu mipepu rege rorumo
    three roads hold three roads  =>  musu nahanapu musu nahanapu sane
    three hills see two ships  =>  musu mipepu hu ragopu munohi

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a boat find a hunter
2. three boats carry two hills
3. two hills find two roads
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a boat find a hunter"  =>  pume gepa gonaro
2. "three boats carry two hills"  =>  musu pumepu hu mipepu rorumo
3. "two hills find two roads"  =>  hu mipepu hu nahanapu gonaro
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the three probes I can infer the language’s core pattern: each sentence is a clause with a subject (boat/hill/road/hunter) + verb (find/carry/watch/hold/see) + object (a boat/hunter/roads/hills). The language has no adjectives, so the only way to express quantity is via a number word (two/three) + noun, and the noun itself is the object. The verb form seems to be a single base form (no tense/aspect markers). The article “the” is used for definite nouns (boat/hunter/roads/hills) but not for indefinite ones (a boat/hunter). The language appears to be a simple SVO with the subject in the first slot, verb in the second, and object in the third.

I’ll probe the verb forms and the handling of the definite article “the” by asking for translations of sentences that use “the” with a noun and also with a number word. I’ll also probe the verb “carry” to see if it behaves like “find” or “watch”.

PROBES:
1. the boat find a hunter
2. the boat carry a hill
3. the king carry three roads
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the boat find a hunter"  =>  pume gepa gonaro
2. "the boat carry a hill"  =>  pume mipe rorumo
3. "the king carry three roads"  =>  mamome musu nahanapu rorumo
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The three probes show that the language has a single verb form for each action (find/carry/watch/hold/see) and that the verb is in the same slot in every sentence. The article “the” is used for definite nouns (boat/hunter/king/roads/hills) but not for indefinite ones (a boat/hunter). The noun itself is the object, and the number word (two/three) is placed before the noun phrase. The language appears to be a simple SVO with the subject in the first slot, verb in the second, and object in the third.

I’ll probe the verb “watch” and the verb “hold” to see if they behave like “find” or “carry”, and I’ll also probe the verb “see” to see if it behaves like “watch”. I’ll also probe the verb “hold” to see if it behaves like “carry”.

PROBES:
1. the king watch three roads
2. the king hold three roads
3. the boat see a hill
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king watch three roads"  =>  mamome musu nahanapu pera
2. "the king hold three roads"  =>  mamome musu nahanapu sane
3. "the boat see a hill"  =>  pume mipe munohi
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The three probes show that the language has a single verb form for each action (watch/hold/see) and that the verb is in the same slot in every sentence. The article “the” is used for definite nouns (king/roads/hill) but not for indefinite ones (a hill). The noun itself is the object, and the number word (three) is placed before the noun phrase. The language appears to be a simple SVO with the subject in the first slot, verb in the second, and object in the third.

I’ll probe the verb “find” with a number word (two) and the verb “carry” with a number word (two) to see if they behave like “find” or “carry”. I’ll also probe the verb “find” with a number word (three) to see if it behaves like “find”.

PROBES:
1. two farmers find two ships
2. three boats carry two hills
3. two hills find two roads
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two farmers find two ships"  =>  hu gisepu hu ragopu gonaro
2. "three boats carry two hills"  =>  musu pumepu hu mipepu rorumo
3. "two hills find two roads"  =>  hu mipepu hu nahanapu gonaro

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. three hunters carry three stones
2. two kings carry three ships
3. two boats carry a hunter
4. two farmers find two boats
5. a hill hold two farmers
6. a king hold a king
7. a hill find two farmers
8. a hunter see three boats
9. a hill find two hunters
10. two stones see two boats

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I have the full grammar from the investigation: the language is a simple SVO with a single verb form for each action (find/carry/watch/hold/see). The article “the” is used for definite nouns (boat/hunter/king/roads/hills) but not for indefinite ones (a boat/hunter). The noun itself is the object, and the number word (two/three) is placed before the noun phrase. The language has no adjectives, so the only way to express quantity is via a number word (two/three) + noun.

I will now translate each of the 10 exam sentences using the grammar I have learned.

ANSWERS:
1. three hunters carry three stones  =>  musu gepapu musu rorumo
2. two kings carry three ships  =>  hu mamome musu ragopu munohi
3. two boats carry a hunter  =>  hu pumepu gepa gonaro
4. two farmers find two boats  =>  hu gisepu hu ragopu pumepu
5. a hill hold two farmers  =>  mipe rege hu gisepu
6. a king hold a king  =>  mamome mamome sane
7. a hill find two farmers  =>  mipe hu gisepu gonaro
8. a hunter see three boats  =>  gepa munohi pumepu
9. a hill find two hunters  =>  mipe gepapu gonaro
10. two stones see two boats  =>  rege munohi pumepu
action.label: response

answers: three hunters carry three stones  =>  musu gepapu musu rorumo
two kings carry three ships  =>  hu mamome musu ragopu munohi
two boats carry a hunter  =>  hu pumepu gepa gonaro
two farmers find two boats  =>  hu gisepu hu ragopu pumepu
a hill hold two farmers  =>  mipe rege hu gisepu
a king hold a king  =>  mamome mamome sane
a hill find two farmers  =>  mipe hu gisepu gonaro
a hunter see three boats  =>  gepa munohi pumepu
a hill find two hunters  =>  mipe gepapu gonaro
two stones see two boats  =>  rege munohi pumepu
